records.notes: Smooth gray-barked tree with alternate toothed leaves and small triangular nuts in prickly husks. This copyback comes from the old beechnut entry and is applied to the consolidated American beech plant record.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food tree.
records.culinary_uses: Nuts are edible and rich when available in good mast years.

records.edibility_detail: Edible nuts, though years of heavy production can vary a lot.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Nuts / Seeds, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: seed / nut / cone. Edible nuts, though years of heavy production can vary a lot.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: American Beech (Fagus grandifolia)

records.notes: Small tree with orange fruits that must be fully soft-ripe before use. This is a legacy southern/eastern fruit record and is not currently proven as a normal wild Upper Michigan foraging target.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit tree in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Very ripe fruits are eaten fresh or used in puddings, breads, and preserves when soft enough to lose their harsh astringency.
records.edibility_detail: Fully soft ripe fruit is edible; unripe fruit is intensely astringent. This record is hidden pending U.P./northern Great Lakes range review.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Range-review record: American persimmon is a useful edible fruit farther south, but wild U.P. foraging relevance is not proven. Do not show as a normal active U.P. card until range proof exists.

records.suppression_reason: Legacy/southern fruit record; wild Upper Michigan foraging relevance not proven.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: American Persimmon (Diospyros virginiana)

records.notes: Large wet-ground Apiaceae with purple-tinged stems and umbrella flower clusters. Correct identification is non-negotiable.
records.culinary_uses: Young stalks and some parts have old traditional use, but this is a caution-heavy edible because carrot-family confusion can go bad fast.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic medicinal plant.
records.edibility_detail: Expert-only Apiaceae entry. Treat stem/leaf use cautiously and only after full positive identification. Do not use angelica based on purple stems, white/green umbels, wet habitat, or aromatic smell alone. Treat root use as historical/traditional context, not a casual food recommendation.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens only. Root use is treated as historical/traditional context, not a casual harvest lane.
records.plant_card_note: Expert-only Apiaceae green/stalk entry. Unknown wet-ground carrot-family plants stay out of the mouth; root use is not a casual harvest recommendation.

records.look_alike_notes: Angelica is a caution-heavy Apiaceae entry. Separate it from poison hemlock and water hemlock using full plant structure, stem surface/blotching, leaf and stalk traits, habitat, and root safety. Also separate it from wild parsnip, which has yellow-green umbels and sap that can cause sunlight-triggered burns.
records.risk_reason: Large wet-ground Apiaceae can be confused with deadly hemlocks if the ID is based only on white/green umbels, divided leaves, purple-toned stems, wet habitat, or aromatic smell.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Tall carrot-family plant, wet habitat, divided leaves, white/greenish umbels, and purple-toned stems can overlap with dangerous hemlock cues.
records.hard_rule: Do not use angelica or any similar wet-ground Apiaceae unless poison hemlock and water hemlock have been ruled out by full plant traits, not by smell, purple tone, or umbrella flowers alone.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly hemlock confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Angelica is a caution-heavy Apiaceae entry. Wet habitat and umbrella-like flowers make poison hemlock and water hemlock comparisons mandatory.
required_checks: Stem hair vs. smoothness check required.
Root/taste checks are not substitutes for full positive identification.
Confirm Angelica structure and aromatic stalk/leaf traits; do not use purple tone alone.
Reject any unknown wetland Apiaceae root.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Unknown wet-ground Apiaceae stays out of the mouth unless the full plant is positively identified.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — nervous-system poisoning and respiratory failure risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly look-alike: purple-spotted smooth hairless stems and mousey odor are poison-hemlock stop signs.
target_traits: Confirmed Angelica structure, not just a tall Apiaceae with umbels.
Large wet-ground plant with Angelica leaf/stalk pattern and aromatic character.
No poison-hemlock-style smooth purple blotched stem pattern.
lookalike_traits: Smooth, hairless, hollow stem instead of a bristly wild-carrot stem.
Purple or reddish blotches/spots on the stem or leaf stalks.
Glossy ferny divided leaves and white umbrella-like flower clusters.
Mousey, musty, or rank odor when crushed.
Deadly poisonous; can affect the nervous and respiratory systems.
required_checks: Check smoothness and purple/red blotching pattern on stems.
Check odor and overall plant structure.
Do not identify from white umbels alone.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Smooth purple-spotted Apiaceae stems are a do-not-eat signal.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly poisonous — seizures and rapid severe poisoning risk.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly wetland look-alike: never use or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
target_traits: Confirmed Angelica structure and safe-use context.
No thick chambered water-hemlock root crown.
Identification is based on the full plant, not wet habitat plus umbels.
lookalike_traits: Wetland, marsh, ditch, shore, or streamside habitat raises the danger level immediately.
White umbels and compound leaves with toothed leaflets can overlap with edible carrot-family plants.
Thick root crown or chambered root structure is an extreme danger sign; roots are especially hazardous.
Do not dig, taste, chew, or smell-test unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.
Deadly poisonous; rapid severe poisoning and seizures are possible.
required_checks: Check wetland root danger before any root/stalk use.
Reject chambered or thick unknown root crowns.
Do not taste wetland Apiaceae roots.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not dig or taste unknown wetland Apiaceae roots.

records.notes: Spiny shrub with small leaves and elongated tart red berries.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, especially associated with roots and bark in older herbal systems.
records.culinary_uses: Tart berries are used in preserves, sauces, and drying rather than carefree fresh eating.
records.edibility_detail: Edible berries, usually best processed.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible berries, usually best processed.

records.notes: Tree with alternate heart-shaped leaves, soft texture when young, and fragrant pale flowers later in season.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing tea tree; flowers are more often the medicinally used part.
records.culinary_uses: Young leaves are mild salad greens, and flowers are commonly used for tea. Tender heart-shaped young leaves are especially good fresh in salads or sandwiches.

records.notes: Trailing evergreen shrub of dry sandy places with leathery leaves and red berries.
records.medicinal_uses: Well-known traditional urinary herb.
records.culinary_uses: Red berries are edible though mealy; leaves also have traditional tea use.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but the fruit is mealy and usually not considered a prime fresh-eating berry.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry, leaf / tea. Edible, but the fruit is mealy and usually not considered a prime fresh-eating berry.

records.notes: Mint-family plant with opposite leaves, square stems, and lavender to pink shaggy flower heads.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea herb for colds, mild sore throat, and general aromatic support.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves can be used fresh or dried for tea and aromatic seasoning.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Flowers, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / tea, flower / tea. Leaves can be used fresh or dried for tea and aromatic seasoning.

records.notes: Recognize the tree first: papery or peeling bark on many species, fine twig structure, and catkins. The app keeps birch as one tree-level entry while describing the useful parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional use centers on sap, bark, and leaf tea as mild tonic or urinary-support material.
records.culinary_uses: Sap can be tapped in late winter to spring for fresh drinking or reduction into syrup; inner bark and cambium have historical famine-food use but sap is the main practical foraging use.

records.edibility_detail: Sap is the main beginner-friendly use. Bark/cambium uses are more situational and should not be confused with routine harvesting.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: sap, bark / cambium, leaf / tea. Sap is the main beginner-friendly use. Bark/cambium uses are more situational and should not be confused with routine harvesting.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: Paper Birch (Betula papyrifera)

records.notes: Tall tree with dark flaky bark on older trunks, drooping racemes of white flowers, and dark purple-black fruits.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional bark use exists, but the fruit is the main food-side legacy interest.
records.culinary_uses: Ripe fruits are used for jelly, syrup, wine, and cooked fruit preparations rather than eaten carelessly by the handful.
records.edibility_detail: Ripe pitted fruit can be used for jelly, syrup, wine, or cooked preparations. Do not eat pits/seeds, leaves, twigs, bark, or wilted foliage. Do not crush pits in blenders or food processors.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Black cherry fruit is the useful part: use ripe, pitted fruit for cooked or processed foods. Pits, seeds, leaves, twigs, bark, and wilted foliage are not food.

use_links.label: NC State Extension: Black Cherry (Prunus serotina)

records.look_alike_notes: Other wild cherries such as chokecherry and pin cherry may be encountered in the region. Treat those as text-only context here unless they are added as separate reviewed records. For this Black Cherry card, confirm Prunus serotina traits: tall tree form, dark flaky mature bark, shiny alternate leaves, white flower racemes, and dark purple-black fruit in drooping clusters.
records.safety_note: Cherry-family pits/seeds and wilted leaves can contain cyanogenic compounds. Use the fruit flesh only after removing pits; do not process whole fruit in a way that cracks pits.

records.notes: Ribes shrub with aromatic foliage and dark fruits borne in clusters.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional berry and leaf use exists, though it is mostly a fruit crop style entry here.
records.culinary_uses: Strongly flavored berries are excellent in jam, syrup, jelly, baking, and juice.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries with a strong currant flavor.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe berries with a strong currant flavor.

records.notes: Arching bramble with whitish cane bloom and hollow-centered black fruits when picked.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a food berry.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh berry also used in jam, syrup, desserts, and preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best fully dark and easily removed from the receptacle.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. Best fully dark and easily removed from the receptacle.

records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry group in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Broad blueberry bucket covering fresh eating, baking, syrup, jam, drying, and freezing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries; flavor and size vary by species and site.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.

records.notes: Low woodland dogwood with whorled leaves, white dogwood-like bracts, and clusters of red berries later in season.
records.culinary_uses: Red berries are edible though mild and are more of a trail nibble or mixed-berry addition than a heavy-yield fruit.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional northern use exists, though it is more often treated as a woodland edible than a major medicinal species.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but mild. Best understood as a small northern woodland fruit rather than a major calorie species.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible, but mild. Best understood as a small northern woodland fruit rather than a major calorie species.

records.notes: Large biennial with huge wavy leaves, woolly undersides, deep taproot, purple thistle-like flowers, and hooked burrs. Root/stalk use is most practical before the plant becomes woody.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional-use plant; do not treat as a medical substitute.
records.culinary_uses: First-year roots can be cooked as a root vegetable; young peeled stalks/flower stalks may be cooked when tender. Avoid old woody roots and contaminated sites.
records.general_notes: Use this record for common burdock / burdock group in U.P. field use. Do not confuse the large leaves with rhubarb as a food cue; rhubarb leaves are toxic.
records.food_quality: Edible root/stalk with site and age caution

images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.

records.edibility_detail: Edible with practical caution. First-year roots are the main target; second-year roots become woody. Avoid roadside, sprayed, or contaminated soil because roots can concentrate site problems.
records.size: Large biennial rosette first year; tall flowering stalk second year.
records.taste: Earthy root vegetable when properly prepared; stalks can be mild when young.
records.plant_lane_note: Edible root/stalk plant with site-contamination and age/texture caution.
records.plant_card_note: First-year roots and young stalks are the useful parts; avoid roadside/sprayed sites.

records.notes: Tall wetland plant with long flat leaves arising in dense basal fans and distinctive sausage-like flower spikes. Clean water matters a lot.
records.medicinal_uses: Some traditional topical use, though mainly an edible wetland staple here.
records.culinary_uses: Shoots, immature flower spikes, pollen, and rhizomes all have food use with proper timing and preparation. Tender inner shoots can be eaten raw or cooked and have a mild cucumber-like to green-corn flavor.

records.edibility_detail: Many edible parts, but harvest timing matters and clean water matters even more.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers, Roots / Tubers.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: stem / shoot / stalk, flower, root / tuber / bulb. Many edible parts, but harvest timing matters and clean water matters even more.

records.notes: Delicate low weed with opposite small leaves, tiny white starry flowers, and a single line of hairs along the stem.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional cooling and soothing herb.
records.culinary_uses: Tender fresh green used in salads, sandwiches, and light cooked dishes.

records.edibility_detail: Edible and best fresh when tender.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Flowers.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, stem / shoot / stalk, flower. Edible and best fresh when tender.

records.notes: Roadside plant with bright blue flowers, stiff branching stems, and a basal rosette in earlier growth.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional digestive bitter and tonic herb, especially focused on the root.
records.culinary_uses: Roots are roasted for a coffee-like flavoring and young leaves can be eaten as bitter greens; flowers are sometimes used decoratively or in small amounts.

records.notes: Shrub with white flowers, shiny leaves, and dark berry clusters that can be quite astringent fresh.

records.culinary_uses: Dark berries are usually processed into juice, jam, wine, syrup, or mixed-fruit preserves rather than eaten as easy fresh fruit.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but usually better processed than eaten plain.

records.notes: This copyback is based on the old red clover entry and applied to the broader clover record in the new app. Common meadow legume with trifoliate leaves and rounded pink to purple flower heads.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional herb for tea and general tonic use.
records.culinary_uses: Flowers and young leaves are used in teas, salads, and dried food blends.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, though often more useful as tea or adjunct food than a main vegetable.

records.image_coverage_note: Representative red clover image added for broad Trifolium/clover record.

records.notes: Opposite leaves when young, smooth stems, white latex sap, and later pink flower clusters. Use only with strong confidence in identification and preparation.
records.culinary_uses: Very young shoots can be boiled in changes of water and eaten as a cooked green by people familiar with proper preparation; this is a preparation-dependent plant, not a casual raw nibble.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional medicinal use exists, but this is a plant that needs careful handling and preparation knowledge.

images_structured.title: Common milkweed plant habit
images_structured.author: Derek Ramsey (Ram-Man)

records.edibility_detail: Not edible raw. Traditional food use requires correct species identification and proper boiling/cooking.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: stem / shoot / stalk, leaf / green. Not edible raw. Traditional food use requires correct species identification and proper boiling/cooking.
